title: Deploying a Highly Available Web Application and Bastion Host in AWS Challenge - Bastion Setup

tasks.title: Sign in to AWS Management Console

steps.text: Click Open Console to launch the AWS Management Console in a new browser tab.
On the login page: Retain the default Account ID (12-digit number; do not edit). Input the provided IAM Username and Password. Click Sign In.
Retain the default Account ID (12-digit number; do not edit).
Input the provided IAM Username and Password.
Click Sign In.
In the Console, select us-east-1 (N. Virginia) from the top-right region dropdown
Sign in to AWS Management Console

XYZ Company wants a web application that stays online even if a server fails, with secure access for updates. Your job is to verify a pre-created network (VPC), set up a bastion host for secure access, launch web servers in multiple zones, create a load balancer to distribute traffic, connect via the bastion, test high availability, and validate the setup. High availability ensures the app works if one server or zone fails, and a bastion host secures server access. Here’s how to do it with key steps and hints:

Check Cloudformation stack is created: Go to Services > CloudFormation > Stacks, look for a stack (e.g., lab-vpc-stack), verify status is CREATE_COMPLETE, note outputs (e.g., VPC ID, public/private subnet IDs for us-east-1a, us-east-1b). Hint: Refresh if the stack isn’t visible.
Go to Services > CloudFormation > Stacks, look for a stack (e.g., lab-vpc-stack), verify status is CREATE_COMPLETE,
note outputs (e.g., VPC ID, public/private subnet IDs for us-east-1a, us-east-1b). Hint: Refresh if the stack isn’t visible.

Create a Bastion Server: Go to EC2 > Launch instances, name it bastion, pick Amazon Linux 2023 kernel AMI, choose t2.micro instance type, select the stack’s public subnet (us-east-1a), enable Auto-assign Public IP, create security group with SSH (port 22)
Go to EC2 > Launch instances, name it bastion, pick Amazon Linux 2023 kernel AMI, choose t2.micro instance type,
select the stack’s public subnet (us-east-1a), enable Auto-assign Public IP,
create security group with SSH (port 22)
Creating a Security Group for the Load Balancer: add inbound rule for HTTP (port 80) from anywhere (0.0.0.0/0), click Create security group.
add inbound rule for HTTP (port 80) from anywhere (0.0.0.0/0), click Create security group.

Steps to create Web-servers: Launch two EC2 instances. For each: Go to EC2 > Launch instances, name them web1 (us-east-1a) and web2 (us-east-1b), pick Amazon Linux 2023 kernel AMI, choose t2.micro instance type, select stack’s private subnets (us-east-1a for web1, us-east-1b for web2), disable public IP, create security group web-sg with HTTP (port 80) from loadbalancer-sg and SSH (port 22) from bastion-sg, Add user data to: Install httpd echo "

Hello from $(hostname)

" > /var/www/html/index.html
Go to EC2 > Launch instances, name them web1 (us-east-1a) and web2 (us-east-1b), pick Amazon Linux 2023 kernel AMI, choose t2.micro instance type,
select stack’s private subnets (us-east-1a for web1, us-east-1b for web2), disable public IP, create security group web-sg with HTTP (port 80) from loadbalancer-sg and SSH (port 22) from bastion-sg,

Add user data to: Install httpd echo "

Hello from $(hostname)

" > /var/www/html/index.html
Install httpd

echo "

Hello from $(hostname)

" > /var/www/html/index.html
Click Launch instance. Hint: Verify both instances are “Running”.
Create a Target Group: Create target group, Select vpc create by stack register both web ec2 as targets
Create target group,
Select vpc create by stack
register both web ec2 as targets
Create a Application Load Balancer: use loadbalancer-sg
use loadbalancer-sg

Connecting to web server via Bastion: SSH to bastion: chmod 400 bastion-key.pem; ssh -i <your-key-pair> ec2-user@<bastion-public-ip>. From bastion, SSH to web1 private IP: ssh ec2-user@<web1-private-ip>, run curl localhost to see “Hello from web1”. Repeat for web2.
SSH to bastion: chmod 400 bastion-key.pem; ssh -i <your-key-pair> ec2-user@<bastion-public-ip>.
From bastion, SSH to web1 private IP: ssh ec2-user@<web1-private-ip>, run curl localhost to see “Hello from web1”. Repeat for web2.
Checking the health of the load balancer: Go to created target group, verify both targets are “Healthy”. Open the ALB DNS name in a browser, see “Hello from web1” or “web2”.
Go to created target group, verify both targets are “Healthy”.
Open the ALB DNS name in a browser, see “Hello from web1” or “web2”.

Test case for High Availability: Go to EC2 > Instances, stop 1st instance, waitt 2-3 minutes, refresh ALB DNS in browser, verify it still loads (from web2). Hint: Check target group health; web1 should be “Unhealthy”.
Go to EC2 > Instances, stop 1st instance, waitt 2-3 minutes, refresh ALB DNS in browser, verify it still loads (from web2).
Hint: Check target group health; web1 should be “Unhealthy”.
In the lab console, click Validate to check the stack, bastion, web servers, load balancer, and high availability.
Hint: Ensure the ALB serves pages after stopping one instance.
Sign out of the AWS Management Console by clicking your username (top-right) and selecting Sign out.
In the lab console, click End Lab from your dashboard and wait for it to finish.
You’ve completed the high availability web application challenge!
